text: The surgeon removes the fallopian tube, carefully excising it from surrounding tissues to prevent future cancers.
The camera focuses on the patient's normal ovary, visible as a round white structure in the background. Follicles are present, indicating the patient is not in menopause.

The surgeon disconnects the uterus at the level of the round ligament, then carefully dissects the bladder from the uterus. A visible fibroid is identified as the cause of the patient's symptoms, necessitating careful separation of the bladder from the uterine tissue.
The surgeon separates the cervix, which will remain with the patient, from the fibroid uterus, using careful dissection techniques.

I have had patients not take any narcotics following their robotic hysterectomy. Of course, everybody reacts differently. Patients decide with their nurse if they would like to stay overnight or to go home immediately from the recovery room. This is not an option for everybody and has to be discussed on an individual basis.
The uterine specimen is completely detached from the cervix, with the cervix remaining intact in the surgical field.
